A YES would therefore require roughly 99 Strategic developments in a 30-day window—an extreme, >10x surge over an already election-year baseline. Evidence before the 2025-10-26 cutoff does show real stress in Honduras: the general election is on 2025-11-30, multiple candidates had already been killed, observers warned about judicial interference and electoral violence, and past contested elections (especially 2017) produced serious unrest. That makes some increase in Strategic developments plausible, especially later around the post-election/transition period. But the threshold is so high that even substantial electoral turmoil may still fall short. So I put very low odds on the two pre-election windows, with a somewhat higher but still clearly low probability for the January window because it could capture post-election disputes and transition friction; the later date is also more uncertain, so I move it somewhat closer to 0.5.

Monaco’s base rate for ACLED-coded riots appears extremely low: the known 360-day reference average as of 2025-10-16 was 0.000, implying no riot events in the prior year, and the background research found only small, apparently peaceful labor demonstrations/strikes rather than violent unrest. Monaco is a tiny, wealthy, tightly policed microstate with low overall crime and no clear active trigger for riot-type mobilization in late 2025. Because the threshold is effectively 'at least one ACLED riot in the 30-day window,' this still leaves a small tail risk from an isolated violent labor protest, spillover from nearby French unrest, or a one-off disturbance. I therefore put the near-term window at the floor-like minimum, then nudge probabilities slightly upward for the later dates to reflect greater uncertainty with forecast horizon.

The known reference value as of 2025-10-16 was exactly 1.000, which strongly suggests ACLED had recorded essentially zero Strategic developments in Lesotho over the prior 360 days. That means the bar for YES is very high: likely 11+ Strategic developments in a 30-day window, and possibly 12+ if the baseline is revised upward slightly. Available pre-2025-10-26 reporting shows political/legal friction around constitutional reforms and court challenges, but not a clear sign of an acute coup, election, or nationwide crisis that would usually be needed to generate a double-digit ACLED Strategic developments burst in such a small country. For 2025-11-02, most of the relevant 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date, and available news did not suggest a late-October flood of such events, so YES looks extremely unlikely. I raise the probability modestly for 2025-11-25 and again for 2026-01-24 to reflect greater uncertainty and tail risk of a renewed constitutional crisis, court rulings, or coalition instability, but the base rate remains very low.

The comparison level is high: the latest known reference is about 128.25 protests per 30 days, so a YES likely requires roughly 129+ ACLED-coded protest events in the window. Recent evidence still points to a very dense protest environment in Israel through October 2025: recurring weekly rallies in Tel Aviv and Jerusalem, nationwide hostage/anti-government mobilizations, Oct. 7 anniversary protests, and rallies around the Oct. 9–13 ceasefire/hostage developments. That makes the 2025-11-02 window slightly more likely than not to beat the elevated baseline. For the later dates, I pull the probabilities back toward 0.5 because the horizon is longer and because a successful ceasefire / major hostage releases could reduce protest intensity versus the already-high prior-year average. Offsetting that, anti-government networks and regular weekly demonstrations appear entrenched, so I keep the later dates just above even rather than below it.

Baseline threshold is about 11.25 ERV events per 30 days (so roughly 12+ events needed). Up to the 2025-10-26 cutoff, eastern DRC still had multiple active drivers of remote violence: persistent M23/FARDC confrontation, continued ceasefire violations, and broader instability in North/South Kivu and nearby provinces. Reuters/ReliefWeb reporting in Sep-Oct 2025 suggests diplomacy was advancing, but fighting had not actually stopped; a monitoring deal existed for an eventual permanent ceasefire, while UN reporting still said peace efforts had not silenced the guns. That makes a near-term above-threshold month somewhat more likely than not. I shade the later horizons closer to 0.5 because (1) the 360-day comparison period already includes very intense months from late 2024/early 2025, making the benchmark nontrivial to beat, and (2) ceasefire/monitoring efforts could gradually dampen shelling and other remote violence over time.

The key threshold is roughly 4 ACLED Protest events in a 30-day window, since the latest known baseline average for the prior 360 days was 3.167. That baseline was likely inflated by the intense Sep 2024-Jan 2025 cost-of-living wave. By March 2025 Reuters reported protests had largely given way to negotiation/concessions, and my searches found little evidence of renewed major protest activity in Martinique in Oct 2025. That makes the near-term window ending 2025-11-02 relatively unlikely to clear 4 events, especially since most of that window was already effectively known by the cutoff. Still, underlying grievances (cost of living, water, anti-colonial sentiment) remain live, and there was at least some labor mobilization in Sep 2025, so future windows retain a meaningful chance of renewed activism. I therefore assign low but rising probabilities with horizon, keeping the longer-dated forecasts closer to 0.5 as uncertainty increases.

The YES threshold is extremely high relative to Hungary’s recent ACLED baseline. The latest known reference value on 2025-10-16 was 3.167, implying a threshold of roughly 32+ Strategic developments in a 30-day window if revisions stay similar. That baseline itself corresponds to only about 2.2 Strategic developments per 30 days over the prior year, so YES would require a very large spike. Recent Hungarian political tension in 2025 (anti-Orban rallies, civil-society crackdown rhetoric, EU rule-of-law disputes, rival October rallies, campaign kickoff) shows elevated political activity, but much of that would likely be coded by ACLED as protests rather than Strategic developments. Nothing in the pre-2025-10-26 information suggests Hungary was already moving toward ~1 Strategic development per day. I allow a small chance of a sharp election-campaign escalation or ACLED coding/revision effects, and I increase uncertainty slightly for the more distant date, but overall YES remains unlikely on all three dates.

So each target mostly reduces to: will ACLED code at least one Riot in Akrotiri or Dhekelia in the relevant 30-day window?

Evidence found points to repeated activism, but mostly peaceful activism: Reuters and other reporting describe January 2024 and September 2024 events at/near RAF Akrotiri as protests/demonstrations with hundreds of participants, police presence, chanting, and no mention of clashes, property destruction, or other behavior that would normally push ACLED from Protest to Riot. For 2025, I found anti-base actions such as protest signage near Akrotiri and planned September demonstrations, but again no clear evidence of violent mob behavior in the base areas. Many actions also occur outside the sovereign base areas proper (for example in Nicosia), which lowers the chance they count for Akrotiri/Dhekelia.

These territories are small, heavily secured, and appear to have a very sparse ACLED riot history. That keeps the probability low. I raise probabilities modestly with horizon because continued Gaza/Yemen-related tensions and recurring anti-base mobilization create some risk of escalation, and per instruction the longer-horizon forecasts should be a bit closer to 0.5.

So: very low for 2025-11-02 because most of that 30-day window is already in the past by the forecast date and no riot evidence surfaced; still low but higher for 2025-11-25; and somewhat higher again by 2026-01-24 due to greater uncertainty and more time for political conditions to change.

Solomon Islands appears to have had essentially zero ACLED 'Battles' in the prior 360 days (the reference value was still 1.000 as of 2025-10-16), so a YES likely requires roughly 11+ battle events in a 30-day window. That is a very high bar for a country whose recent instability has mostly taken the form of protests, riots, and political unrest rather than sustained armed clashes between organized armed groups. Available pre-cutoff reporting points to latent ethnic/political tensions and limited police capacity, but also to strong preventive security presence and no major post-election violence in 2024. So the base rate for ACLED battles is extremely low, with only a small tail risk of sudden militia/landowner/security-force clashes. I therefore assign a floor-level probability for 2025-11-02, rising modestly for the later windows as forecast uncertainty increases.

The key anchor is the prompt’s own reference value: as of 2025-10-16, Azerbaijan’s 360-day trailing 30-day-average fatalities in ACLED was 0.500. That strongly suggests the effective threshold on all three dates is very likely ‘at least 1 fatality in the prior 30 days’ (it would take a large late backfill to push the threshold to 2). Reuters reporting before the cutoff indicates no Armenia-Azerbaijan border deaths since Feb. 2024 despite recurring ceasefire-violation claims, which argues against frequent battle fatalities. The main residual risk appears to be intermittent landmine/UXO deaths in recovered territories; a September 2025 Aghdam landmine death shows that risk remained live near the forecast date. For 2025-11-02, most of the relevant 30-day window had already elapsed by the cutoff, and searches did not turn up an obvious October fatality, so I put this below the later dates but not extremely low because ACLED/local-source events can be missed before publication. For the later two dates, a reasonable base rate from the reference value is roughly a 0.4-ish chance of at least one ACLED-coded fatality in any given 30-day window, with the farthest date pulled slightly closer to 0.5 to reflect greater uncertainty.

Baseline ACLED activity is extremely low: the stated reference value of 1.083 implies roughly ~1 explosion/remote-violence event in the prior 360 days, so the effective threshold is about 11 events in a 30-day window. For Canada, that would require an extraordinary jump far above recent levels. Pre-cutoff reporting suggests isolated firebombing/incendiary incidents and some foiled plots, but not anything close to a sustained wave of 11+ such events in a month. Official Canadian terrorism threat assessments remained at Medium through 2025, indicating real but not exceptional attack risk. The 2025-11-02 window is especially unlikely because most of that 30-day period had already elapsed by the cutoff with no visible sign of a major surge. I raise the probability modestly for later dates only because uncertainty grows with horizon and there is some tail risk of escalation, but the event still appears unlikely overall.

The key number is the threshold: with the most recent known reference value at 10.5, a YES would likely require roughly 106+ ACLED 'Violence against civilians' events in Venezuela in a 30-day window. That is extremely high relative to the apparent recent baseline. Since 10.5 = 1 + (360-day total / 12), it implies about 114 VAC events over the prior 360 days, or about 9.5 per 30 days on average. So the question asks whether Venezuela will jump from a recent norm around 10/month to more than 10 times that level in a single month. 

Available pre-cutoff reporting shows continued repression in Venezuela, including killings, disappearances, arbitrary detentions, and intimidation after the 2024 election and through 2025. But I found no evidence that recent periods were anywhere near 100+ VAC events in 30 days. The big immediate post-election unrest of July-August 2024 is mostly outside the baseline window anyway, and even 2025 election-related reporting suggests repression persisted without clear signs of a nationwide event count explosion on the scale needed here. 

So near-term resolution by 2025-11-02 looks very unlikely; 2025-11-25 is still unlikely but allows a bit more room for deterioration; 2026-01-24 gets a modestly higher probability because unforeseen political shocks could accumulate over a longer horizon. Still, absent evidence of an imminent national breakdown, all three remain low-probability YES outcomes.

The threshold is roughly 31 fatalities in a 30-day window (based on the known 31.167 reference as of 2025-10-16, likely close to the final 2025-10-26 baseline). As of the forecast date, Bangladesh had elevated but not obviously exploding violence: HRSS reported a four-year high in political violence (160 killed in Sep 2024-Sep 2025), continued mob/inter-party clashes, and some added tail risk from Rohingya militant spillover near the border. That said, the baseline already embeds an elevated year, and I do not see strong evidence by 2025-10-26 of an imminent July-2024-scale mass-fatality surge that would make going over 31 much more likely than not. So I lean only slightly above even odds for the nearest window, with probabilities moving closer to 0.5 for the later dates as uncertainty increases and mean reversion becomes more plausible.

Baseline is effectively ~1.0, so the practical threshold is about 11+ ACLED fatalities in a 30-day window. Mongolia has had near-zero ACLED fatalities over the prior year, and even major 2025 anti-government protests that toppled the PM appear to have produced no reported deaths. Recent monitoring snippets did not flag Mongolia for deadly unrest, and Mongolia’s notable historical deadly unrest (e.g. 2008 election riots) was rare and still only about 5 deaths, below the likely threshold. So a YES would require an unusually sharp, atypical escalation. I therefore put low odds on the near dates, with modestly higher odds farther out because uncertainty increases with horizon and there is more time for political/economic stress to generate a rare violent episode.

The comparison threshold is high but not extreme: about 435 protest events in a 30-day period, based on the latest pre-cutoff reference. That bar was lifted by the huge March 2025 anti-government wave, so beating it requires sustained nationwide activity, not just isolated rallies. Still, the best pre-2025-10-26 evidence points to protest activity remaining elevated into the fall: Reuters reported a major Ankara rally on Sept. 14, clashes/rallies around CHP offices on Sept. 7, and by Oct. 15 described ongoing weekly 'Freedom for Imamoglu' demonstrations across Istanbul districts. The months-long legal crackdown on the CHP suggests continued mobilization pressure.

So for 2025-11-02, most of the 30-day window is already visible and includes this weekly protest campaign, giving a modest YES lean. For 2025-11-25, persistence into November seems somewhat more likely than not, but without a specific known trigger and with repression possibly damping turnout, only a slight edge. For 2026-01-24, horizon uncertainty is higher and winter/holiday seasonality may slightly reduce protest volume, so I move close to even.

Baseline risk is low: the latest known 360-day rolling 30-day average for Chilean ACLED 'Explosions/Remote violence' was about 0.083, which implies the effective threshold is almost certainly at least 1 event in the target 30-day window. That also suggests only about one such ACLED-coded event in the prior year, so these events are rare. I found evidence of one clear 2025 qualifying-type incident (the March 2025 school firebomb explosion), plus October 2025 protest-anniversary alerts and reporting that Molotovs/fireworks/barricades are common in unrest periods. However, much Chile unrest is more likely to be coded by ACLED as protests/riots/arson rather than 'Explosions/Remote violence,' limiting the uplift. For 2025-11-02, most of the relevant 30-day window had already elapsed by the cutoff and I found no clear reported qualifying incident, so I put this only slightly above the floor. For 2025-11-25 and 2026-01-24, I allow a modest increase for future uncertainty and ongoing unrest/election-period volatility, while keeping probabilities low because the category remains uncommon in Chile.

Threshold is likely about 11 strategic-development events in a 30-day window, because the reference value near 2025-10-26 was 1.083 (= roughly 1 strategic-development event over the prior 360 days, divided by 12, plus 1). That is a high bar relative to the recent baseline. The evidence up to 2025-10-26 shows a genuine political crunch in New Caledonia — Bougival deal fallout, FLNKS rejection, election-postponement votes in Congress and the French Senate, parliamentary delays, and warnings of renewed instability (search_2_summary, search_3_summary). But the number of clearly codable ACLED strategic developments visible in late Sep–late Oct still looks more like several events over ~5 weeks than an obvious 11+ in 30 days. For 2025-11-02, much of the 30-day window was already effectively underway by the cutoff, and it seems unlikely that the count had already reached the high threshold or would surge enough in the final week. For 2025-11-25, the approach to the election/legal deadline makes a spike more plausible, so I raise the probability. For 2026-01-24, there is more scenario uncertainty (possible post-deadline disputes, institutional implementation, or renewed unrest versus a lull), so I move somewhat closer to 0.5 per horizon, but the threshold still keeps the probability below even.

The effective hurdle is likely about 5 strategic-development events in a 30-day window, since the latest known 360-day monthly average for Ivory Coast on 2025-10-16 was 4.750 (though ACLED backfill could move this a bit). My main update is the very high-salience October 2025 election: excluded opposition leaders, boycott/shutdown calls, election-security preparations, the 25 Oct vote itself, and the near-certain stream of result/certification reactions in the following days all make the window ending 2025-11-02 more likely than not to clear the bar. For the window ending 2025-11-25, some immediate post-election strategic events should still occur (results, legal/political reactions, possible security measures), but the most intense pre-election period drops out, so I put it only modestly above even. By late Dec 2025-Jan 2026, absent a larger crisis, activity likely mean-reverts toward a normal month; with baseline already elevated by the election year, I put that window slightly below even. Key uncertainty is ACLED coding/backfill: some election-related incidents may be coded as protests/riots rather than strategic developments.

The key fact is that the reference value was 1.000 as of 2025-10-16, implying essentially zero recorded Antarctica 'Battles' over the prior 360 days; even if ACLED revises slightly by 2025-10-26, the YES threshold is still likely about 11+ battles in a 30-day window. Antarctica is extraordinarily low-risk for ACLED battles: tiny population, treaty-constrained demilitarization, and very little evidence of organized armed-group activity there. The notable 2025 Sanae IV incident was an alleged assault within a research team, which does not fit ACLED's 'Battles' definition of violent interaction between two organized armed groups. So a YES resolution would require a sudden and extreme breakdown—e.g., interstate or organized-group militarized clashes producing double-digit battle events in one month—which seems very unlikely. I therefore assign the minimum allowed probability near-term, with only a modest increase for the longer horizons.

Primary anchor is the FRED tool’s estimate for the near date: with the last pre-cutoff value at 1.65 on 2025-10-24, the model gave a combined P(2025-11-02 > 1.65) of 0.52. I nudge that slightly upward because the spread had fallen to the low end of its recent range and the linear fit projects a modest rebound by early November. The broader context from pre-cutoff reporting is mixed: Q3 2025 spreads had tightened to very low levels and strong fund/foreign demand was supporting credit, which limits confidence in a widening call, but strategists also highlighted elevated event/recession/tariff risk and a defensive posture, which makes a modest re-widening somewhat plausible. So I land only slightly above even odds for 11/02. For 11/25 and especially 2026-01-24, I move probabilities closer to 0.5 to reflect greater horizon uncertainty, while keeping a small residual tilt toward YES because the starting level appears relatively compressed versus the recent 2025 range.

Primary anchor is the FRED series tool: as of 2025-10-24 the AA corporate yield was 4.38, with the tool’s combined estimate giving a 0.62 chance that the next short-horizon value exceeds 4.38 by 2025-11-02. Because 2025-10-26 is a Sunday, the effective comparison point is likely very close to the 10/24 value, so that anchor is directly relevant. Recent data show the series has fallen into the low end of its past-year range, which slightly favors a rebound/up-move. The one web search also points to a modestly upward rates backdrop into late 2025: Reuters reported expectations for long Treasury yields to stay elevated, while IG corporate spreads were described as tight, implying spreads may not add much but Treasury yields could nudge all-in AA yields somewhat higher. Offsetting that, very recent momentum in the series was downward and a Reuters Oct. 23 piece highlighted a fresh Treasury-yield drop after Powell comments. Net: I lean YES for all three dates, but with uncertainty increasing over time, so probabilities move closer to 0.5 at longer horizons.

Primary anchor: the FRED time-series tool shows the BBB effective yield at 4.88 on 2025-10-24, very close to the likely 2025-10-26 comparison level, and gives a combined estimate of P(value > 4.88 by 2025-11-02) = 0.65. I use that as the main prior for the first date. Supplementary late-October reporting suggests long Treasury yields were around 4.0% and expected by strategists to stay elevated or drift slightly higher over coming months because inflation remained sticky, term premium was high, and fiscal pressures persisted. Since BBB corporate yields are roughly Treasury yield plus a credit spread, that creates a modest upward bias from the current low level. Offsetting this, credit spreads were still tight and the Fed was cutting, so a rally / lower yield scenario remained plausible. Net: modest lean to YES for all dates, but with increasing horizon uncertainty, so the later probabilities move closer to 0.5.

As of 2025-10-26, no post-May 1 measure clearly satisfies the question yet. The January 2025 AI Diffusion Rule created broad controls on advanced chips and model weights, but BIS rescinded/non-enforced it in May before compliance, and the May 2025 BIS actions were mainly guidance/policy statements, which appear interpretive and/or targeted rather than clearly new broad export controls. So the question mostly turns on whether BIS issues a replacement rule before 2025 ends. Evidence in hand points to that being more likely than not: BIS officially said in May it would replace the rescinded rule, and a September 2025 regulatory-agenda report said BIS was aiming to issue an October rule titled 'Framework for Secure Sharing of Advanced AI Technology with Trusted U.S. Partners Worldwide,' suggesting a broad successor covering advanced AI tech/chips and possibly related controls. National-security pressure and bipartisan support also favor action. Offsetting this, the rule had still not appeared by the Oct. 26 cutoff, prior target dates had already slipped, and the topic is diplomatically and technically complex, so delay into 2026 remains substantial. Net: modestly favor YES.

As of 2025-10-26, no qualifying federal AI-regulation law had been enacted, and the strongest enacted/de facto candidate discussed in coverage—the TAKE IT DOWN Act—appears excluded by the question background because it regulates publication of certain content rather than AI systems or development itself. Other plausible candidates (especially the NO FAKES Act, plus newer proposals like the AI LEAD Act and various deepfake or standards bills) had bipartisan interest but had not cleared both chambers by the cutoff, and some had already stalled previously. With only about two months remaining in 2025, Congress’s calendar was tight, attention was reportedly dominated by shutdown/NDAA issues, and late-introduced broad AI bills faced very long odds. There is still a nontrivial chance of a narrow AI-specific provision hitching a ride on year-end legislation, but overall the base rate and timeline make enactment unlikely.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the coalition had advanced planning and substantial political buy-in: Macron said 26 countries had pledged to contribute forces on land/sea/air, and official statements said plans were in place for a Multinational Force Ukraine. But the key point is that these same statements consistently conditioned deployment on hostilities having ceased / after a ceasefire or peace deal. As of Oct. 24, Putin had rejected a ceasefire and active fighting continued. So a YES by 2025-12-31 likely requires: (1) a ceasefire or equivalent halt in hostilities within about two months, and then (2) rapid implementation of a politically sensitive multinational mission that still appeared to need U.S. backing and final national approvals. That path remained possible but distinctly unlikely. I therefore put the probability at 12%.

Official pre-cutoff evidence strongly indicates the condition has already been met. The UK government officially announced on 6 June 2022 that it would gift M270 MLRS to Ukraine, and on 11 August 2022 announced additional M270 systems and guided missiles (search_0_summary). M270 is explicitly a HIMARS-compatible rocket artillery system under the question’s criteria. Germany also publicly committed in June 2022 to provide MARS II launchers, another M270-family system (search_0_summary; Reuters result in search #1). Because the resolution criterion only requires that at least one European government or the EU formally announce such a commitment by 31 Dec 2025, these earlier formal announcements appear to satisfy it already. Remaining uncertainty is mainly about any hidden interpretive wrinkle over whether prior commitments count, but on the plain wording they should.

I estimate a low chance because the available 2025 volume data imply Q1–Q3 were well below the 15 bcm threshold, so the question is basically about whether Q4 2025 alone could surge that high. Evidence gathered: (1) Bruegel-linked reporting says total Russian gas deliveries to the EU were about 19.3 bcm in H1 2025, making it effectively impossible for either Q1 or Q2 to have reached 15 bcm; (2) Bruegel/TASS-based reporting says total EU imports from Russia were about 27.6 bcm in the first nine months of 2025, implying Q3 was only about 8.3 bcm; (3) pipeline supply is now limited to TurkStream, with Reuters citing roughly 4.5 bcm through its European section in Q1 and CREA citing 13.8 bcm via TurkStream in Q1–Q3, so pipeline volumes alone cannot drive a 15 bcm quarter; (4) reaching 15 bcm in Q4 would therefore require an unusually large LNG spike, far above the 2025 run rate. Winter demand, lack of an immediate EU ban, and some member-state opposition to restrictions keep the probability above zero, but the threshold looks materially above what the 2025 data trend supports.

As of the cutoff, Musk looks like the favorite but not a lock. Dated reporting before 2025-10-26 indicates: (1) Larry Ellison briefly overtook Musk on Sept. 10 after Oracle’s huge jump, showing the ranking can flip on market moves; but (2) by late September Musk had regained #1; and (3) Reuters/BBC reports from Oct. 1-2 had Musk clearly back in front on Bloomberg’s measure, roughly $459B vs. Ellison around $351B, with other October reporting still showing Musk ahead by a large margin. So the state near the cutoff is: Musk is currently #1, with an ~85B-150B cushion over the main challenger, and only about two months remain. That said, this is still a Bloomberg daily ranking driven by volatile asset prices, especially Tesla and Oracle, and September proved that a single sharp move can change the order. Netting those together, Musk is favored to remain #1 on Dec. 31, 2025, but there is still meaningful upset risk.

As of 2025-10-26, Iran had repeatedly threatened to close or restrict the Strait in June and again in October, especially in response to U.S./allied sanctions and possible enforcement actions, and parliament had backed closure in June. But the strongest evidence cuts the other way: during the much more acute June 2025 U.S.-Israel-Iran crisis, Iran still did not actually move to disrupt Strait shipping, and CRS reporting said Iran did not appear to have actively attempted disruption then. MARAD advisories in August still cited seizure/boarding risk but no specific threat, and there was no evidence by late October of an actual halt or severe restriction of traffic. Closing the Strait would also damage Iran’s own exports and key partners like China, creating a strong deterrent. The remaining horizon is only about two months, so while escalation or selective interdiction of Western shipping is plausible, it looks meaningfully less likely than not.

As of 2025-10-26, this looks unlikely to resolve YES. The strongest evidence is that even during the severe April–May 2025 crisis—described as the worst/heaviest fighting in decades, involving missiles, drones, air strikes, and dozens of deaths—neither India nor Pakistan issued a formal declaration of war (search_0_summary, search_1_summary). Instead, both accepted a May 10 ceasefire, pursued de-escalation, and by late May were reducing troops; Reuters also reported in August that India said there had been no ceasefire violation along the LoC (search_1_summary, search_3_summary). The resolution criteria are unusually strict: border fighting, strikes, and proxy conflict do not count; only an official public declaration does. That matters a lot because India-Pakistan conflicts have historically often been fought without formal declarations, making a declaration a higher bar than 'serious hostilities.' With only about two months left in 2025 after the cutoff, the base rate for such a formal step is very low. I leave a small tail risk for a major new terrorist attack or sudden escalation that prompts an explicit declaration, but overall the chance is low.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Musk was still clearly Tesla CEO, participating in the Oct. 23 Q3 earnings call. The strongest late evidence points toward continuity, not departure: Tesla’s board had approved a roughly $29B interim award intended to keep him leading through at least 2030, and in September-October 2025 it proposed an even larger long-term package explicitly framed as retaining and incentivizing him to stay at the helm (search_3_summary). Earlier 2025 reporting did show real strain: the board reportedly discussed succession/search options and asked Musk to spend more time on Tesla, while investors pressed for succession planning and criticized governance (search_1_summary, search_2_summary). But by the fall, the board’s observable actions were strongly pro-Musk, and analysts described replacing him as highly risky because Tesla is so tied to his brand (search_2_summary). With only about two months from cutoff to resolution, a CEO change would likely require an acute trigger—resignation in a conflict, sudden forced removal, or death/incapacity. Those are materially possible given Tesla governance drama, but still clearly less likely than Musk remaining CEO through year-end. Net estimate: 12% YES.

As of 2025-10-26, Tesla had launched ride-hailing in Austin and obtained a Texas permit that allows operation without an onboard safety driver, so the path to YES was real. But the market’s bar is much stricter than a simple launch: at least 10,000 bona fide rides, no human observer in the car, no human backup/teleoperation dependence, and still active on Dec. 31. Mainstream reporting through late October still described Tesla as planning to remove safety monitors only by year-end or ‘within a few months,’ implying qualifying fully driverless service was not yet clearly established. New markets were also expected to begin with human attendants, making Austin the main plausible source of qualifying rides. With only about two months left, Tesla needed both a genuine no-human-backup deployment and rapid scaling to 10,000 rides. Given Tesla’s progress and permissive Texas regulation, this was possible, but the combination of strict criteria, limited evidence of already-qualifying rides, and Tesla’s history of optimistic autonomy timelines kept the probability low.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the evidence strongly favors YES. The official 2024 total fertility rate already rebounded to 0.75 from 0.72 in 2023. For 2025, available in-year indicators were still positive: first-half births were up 7.4% y/y, Q1 total fertility rate was reported at 0.82, and June’s monthly fertility rate was 0.76, with marriages continuing to rise for over a year. Officials were forecasting roughly 0.79 for full-year 2025 and >250,000 births. Since the bar to clear is only 0.72, 2025 would need an unexpectedly sharp late-year collapse and/or major revision to finish below 2023, which looks unlikely given the broad-based rebound and marriage-to-birth lag. I leave some room for uncertainty because monthly/quarterly fertility figures can be noisy and official annual data were not yet final by the cutoff.

As of 2025-10-26, Musk is still clearly acting as Tesla CEO: he led the late-October earnings call, Tesla’s board and proxy materials explicitly back him, and Tesla approved/advanced huge compensation packages designed to keep him in a top executive role for years (search_1_summary, search_3_summary). That makes a departure in the remaining ~2 months of 2025 unlikely. 

There is some real risk, though: the May report that the board had explored succession planning, continuing governance criticism, Delaware/pay-package litigation, proxy-firm opposition, investor frustration, and the upcoming Nov. 6 shareholder meeting all show nontrivial pressure (search_2_summary). Also, Musk has reportedly contemplated stepping aside before. 

Still, given his control/influence over Tesla, the board’s public support, and the short remaining horizon, I judge the chance he ceases to be CEO by 2025-12-31 to be low but not negligible.

By 2025-10-26, the strongest evidence for YES is that the US and Iran had already crossed the direct-use-of-force threshold in June 2025: US strikes on Iranian nuclear sites, an Iranian retaliatory strike on a US base in Qatar, and then a rapid ceasefire/de-escalation. That shows the pathway to escalation is real. Late-September/October snapback UN sanctions, failed nuclear diplomacy, worsening Iranian economic pressure, and continued US threats of force also keep some tail risk alive.

But the resolution bar is higher than ‘another strike happens.’ The creator has clarified that limited targeted strikes, an assassination, or a single headline are not enough; the conflict must look like an actively and sustainably prosecuted US-Iran war. As of late October there was no sustained campaign, no significant US ground deployment, no major US declaration of active war, and Reuters/AP reporting instead emphasized sanctions, pressure, and deadlocked diplomacy rather than ongoing war. June 2025 itself appears to have fallen short of the market’s YES threshold, which implies that even another brief exchange might still resolve NO.

With only about two months left in the forecast horizon, I think a war severe and sustained enough to satisfy these criteria is possible but clearly unlikely. Hence 13%.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, San Diego FC had already officially clinched an MLS playoff berth and, per multiple contemporaneous reports, finished as the No. 1 seed in the Western Conference with 63 points. Sources before the cutoff explicitly described them as having earned home-field advantage and listed their first-round playoff series. Since the question is whether they make the playoffs in 2025, and qualification was already secured before the forecast date, this is effectively already determined. I use 0.95 rather than higher only because the instructions cap confidence away from certainty.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the answer looks overwhelmingly NO. NASA’s only credible near-term crewed lunar landing program, Artemis III, had already been officially delayed well past 2025—first to no earlier than September 2026, then to mid-2027—and Artemis II, a prerequisite crewed flyby, had not yet flown and was targeting early 2026 at the soonest (search_0_summary, search_2_summary). On top of that, NASA was publicly discussing SpaceX HLS delays and even reopening lunar lander competition in October 2025, which is evidence of slippage rather than acceleration (search_3_summary). China’s stated crewed lunar target remained around 2030, not 2025, and no other nation or private program had a credible path to land humans on the Moon within the remaining two months. Realistically the true chance is extremely close to zero, but per the required probability bounds I submit the floor value of 5%.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the evidence strongly suggests TSLA had not yet traded below $200 in 2025. The spring selloff was severe: Reuters and other sources indicate the stock fell roughly 50% from its December 2024 high, with April prices around the low $230s, and technicians were watching support near $215 and then $165. But later reports show a recovery: about $275 in early June, about $292 in early July, and near 2025 highs by mid-September, with late-October moves still centered well above $200. So this forecast is mostly about the remaining ~2 months of 2025. A drop below $200 would likely require another roughly 30-40% decline from late-October levels. That is not impossible for TSLA given its extreme volatility, rich valuation, profit misses, margin pressure, and sentiment risk around Musk/politics. But with the stock having already absorbed a lot of bad news in 2025 and still rebounded, sub-$200 by year-end looks more like a tail scenario than the base case.

Trump winning is already effectively satisfied by the October 2025 info, so the question is mainly whether BLS headline inflation will be under 2.5% by the relevant December 2025 release. As of the cutoff, CPI was 2.9% YoY in August and 3.0% YoY in September, with Cleveland Fed nowcasts around 3.0% into October and broad economist consensus expecting inflation to stay around or above 3% through late 2025. Tariff pass-through is a major upward pressure, and several forecasters expected Q4 inflation to rise further, not fall. From 3.0% in September, getting below 2.5% by early December would require a very sharp disinflation in just 1-2 reports, which looks possible only via a sizable downside surprise (for example from energy or measurement issues), but not likely.

Trump’s 2025 Nobel Peace Prize outcome is already fixed as NO, so this market now resolves YES iff there is no bilateral Ukraine ceasefire before 2026. As of 2025-10-26, active diplomacy still exists, but the latest Reuters reporting says Russia rejected an immediate ceasefire, keeps demanding territorial concessions first, and even a follow-up Trump-Putin summit was on hold. Earlier forecasting aggregates (RAND/Glimt/Metaculus) already leaned against a 2025 ceasefire, and conditional on reaching late October with no deal and major terms still unresolved, the remaining chance of a bilateral ceasefire by Dec. 31 looks modest rather than central. I still leave meaningful room for a sudden diplomatic breakthrough, but the more likely outcome is no ceasefire, hence YES on this IFF market.

By 2025-10-26, I found no evidence that SNL had already done a Waymo sketch in 2025 despite targeted searches. That matters because if it had happened, it likely would be easy to find. From the cutoff to year-end, SNL appears to have only a limited number of episodes left (roughly the November/December run), so there are only a handful of remaining chances. The case for YES is that Waymo is culturally salient in 2025: rapid expansion, large LA presence, and the high-profile Los Angeles protest/fire incidents made it a recognizable news object and a plausible comedy premise (search_4_summary). SNL also does transportation/rideshare sketches. But the case against is stronger: Waymo is still somewhat geographically niche, many competing topics will crowd the remaining episodes, and SNL could do a self-driving-car joke without naming Waymo, which would not count. Net: plausible but clearly below even odds.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Musk had clearly announced the ‘America Party’ in July and major media described it as a new party, but the more concrete evidence for actual establishment remained weak. Multiple reports said the FEC filings circulating right after launch were fake; later reporting (Axios, Business Insider, Reuters/WSJ) said he had not taken visible formal steps, had no clear official website or dedicated official party infrastructure, and may have cooled on the idea to refocus on his companies. Given the market’s clarified criteria—that a tweet alone is not enough and some tangible evidence / media consensus of a real party is needed—the base case is NO as of the cutoff. There were only about two months left in 2025, which is enough time for Musk to change his mind and create a website/account or otherwise formalize it, but the lack of progress for months after the July announcement makes that relatively unlikely. I therefore put YES at 17%.

Iran faced unusually high stress by 2025-10-26: war with Israel, renewed sanctions, recession/hyperinflation risk, elite infighting, and concerns about Khamenei’s health/succession. Those factors clearly raised instability risk. But the strongest near-term evidence still pointed to regime survival through year-end: Reuters reported no significant anti-regime protests after the June war, the state had shifted into an effective internal crackdown, opposition groups remained divided and many activists were wary of mobilizing during external conflict, and analyses still described the IRGC/security apparatus as broadly loyal and cohesive. Crucially, the resolution requires the regime to lose control of the central government in Tehran; unrest, border insurgency, or even a managed succession inside the Islamic Republic would resolve NO. With only about two months left in 2025, the window for a full regime collapse was short. So I assign a meaningfully elevated but still low probability: about 11%.

As of 2025-10-26, the evidence strongly suggests hallucinations are improving but not ‘fixed.’ Multiple late-2025 sources still describe hallucinations as an active problem, especially in reasoning/agentic settings; OpenAI’s own 2025 reports showed some newer reasoning models hallucinating more on certain benchmarks. GPT-5 and other frontier models appear to reduce hallucination rates substantially on some tests, with best-case grounded benchmarks around ~0.8–1.3%, but that is mitigation, not elimination, and several sources explicitly note lower lab rates do not mean trustworthy real-world behavior. Since the market resolves YES only if the evaluator judges the hallucination problem has been solved by end-2025, and there were only ~2 months left from the cutoff with no sign of a decisive breakthrough, NO looked overwhelmingly more likely. I leave a small chance for a subjective resolution based on dramatic late-2025 progress and optimistic interpretation of ‘fixed.’

As of 2025-10-26, the lawsuit appears active and contested rather than nearing resolution. Independent docket summaries show the case was filed in Feb. 2025, remained in discovery/protective-order fights through at least Sept. 2025, had a June evidentiary hearing, and then saw a Sept. 4 order allowing amendment followed by an amended complaint on Sept. 5. I found no sign of a notice of settlement, dismissal, or publicly reported agreement by the cutoff. Base rates for civil cases favor eventual settlement, but the relevant horizon is only about two months after the cutoff. The late-stage amendment, ongoing motion practice, and public/personal nature of the dispute suggest meaningful friction and reduce the chance of a quick deal by year-end. Offsetting that, discovery pressure and likely settlement discussions/mediation in federal civil cases still make a 2025 settlement plausible. Net: below even odds, but far from negligible.

Nigeria reaching 95% of adults with a bank/financial institution account before 2025 looks extremely unlikely. The strongest pre-cutoff evidence points the other way: EFInA/CBN reporting for 2023 says only about 52% of adults had bank accounts, while formal financial inclusion overall was about 64%, with 47% still lacking transactional bank accounts and 26% fully excluded (search_1_summary, search_2_summary, search_3 results). To resolve YES, Nigeria would have needed an extraordinary jump of roughly 30-40+ percentage points in a single year, far beyond recent progress. NIBSS data showing 200M+ active bank accounts are not unique-person counts and are explicitly accompanied by much lower BVN counts (~63M mid-2024; ~65M by early 2025), which is more consistent with the survey evidence and still far below 95% of Nigerian adults. Also, if no convincing official data emerges by the deadline, the market resolves NO by the stated criteria. So YES is possible only via a major measurement surprise, not via the visible trend.

As of 2025-10-26 there is strong political momentum against solar geoengineering (CBD moratorium reaffirmed, AMCEN rejection, ~60 Global South countries reportedly backing non-use negotiations), but I found no evidence of an actual binding diplomatic agreement with 10+ countries already in force or signed. The main existing instruments are generally described as nonbinding COP decisions or political calls, not treaty obligations specific enough to satisfy the resolution. With only about two months left, and after UNEA-6 and CBD processes showed both momentum and strong opposition/blocking by key states, the chance of crossing from advocacy/political declarations to a qualifying treaty-level commitment before 2026 looks low. Residual probability reflects ambiguity in how adjudicators might treat existing treaty-body decisions or a late-2025 regional/plurilateral agreement.

By 2025-10-26, the evidence points strongly against a YES resolution before 2026. The main peer-reviewed result is Loeb et al. in Chemical Geology (Sept. 2024), which argues some IM1-site spherules have unusual/undetermined compositions, but it does not establish with 95% confidence that they are non-Earth in origin, and it does not come close to showing a technological origin with no known natural explanation. A peer-reviewed rebuttal in Chemical Geology (Jan. 2025) argues most of the spherules are volcanic/terrestrial, directly undercutting the required non-Earth conclusion. I found no evidence by the cutoff of a published multi-lab systematic review concluding both extraterrestrial and technological origin, nor of a completed second expedition yielding larger decisive fragments. With only about two months left until the publication deadline, and with the current literature still centered on disputed geochemistry rather than strong technological evidence, a YES outcome looks extremely unlikely. I assign the floor-like probability of 5%.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the evidence points strongly toward NO. By Q3 2025 the S&P 500 had already recovered from its early-year selloff, hit 28 all-time highs, and was up about 14.8% YTD, with a max drawdown of only 18.9% (search_3_summary). Reporting through Sept./Oct. 2025 says the Magnificent Seven still accounted for a huge share of index gains and that none were reported anywhere near the question’s threshold except Tesla, which had at one point fallen about 50% from its high; the others were generally down far less, with early-2025 declines mostly in the ~7-15% range and Tesla the standout loser (search_0_summary, search_3_summary). With only about two months left until 2026, YES would require at least three more of Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Nvidia, and Meta to suffer 50% drawdowns from ATHs in a very short window. Given their late-2025 recovery / near-high status and the extremity of the threshold, that would likely need a severe market crash or company-specific collapses across several names at once. That tail risk is not zero, but it looks quite low.

As of the cutoff, the best-known U.S. headline estimate is still roughly 35k annual deaths from antibiotic-resistant infections (CDC 2019 figure, still echoed in 2024 federal materials). I found no clear pre-cutoff CDC or major-source U.S. report already giving a 70k+ headline number. Recent global/Lancet-style AMR work points to long-run increases, but on anything like the published trajectory the burden rises gradually, not to a clean doubling by 2025. So the main YES path is not a real near-term doubling, but a late-2025 publication or CDC update using a broader methodology (for example, a more expansive or 'associated with AMR' framing) that crosses 70k. That is plausible, but with only weeks left after the cutoff and no strong sign of such a report, it looks distinctly unlikely.

Para el 26 de octubre de 2025, la evidencia clave es que Rockstar/Take-Two anunció oficialmente el 2 de mayo de 2025 que Grand Theft Auto VI se retrasa al 26 de mayo de 2026, abandonando la ventana de otoño de 2025. Múltiples fuentes independientes antes del cutoff recogen el mismo anuncio oficial, y no hay indicios de un lanzamiento regional anticipado en Europa ni de una venta limitada autorizada en 2025. Dado que la resolución exige que esté a la venta en Europa dentro de 2025, el escenario base es claramente NO; dejo un 5% residual solo por la pequeña posibilidad de una situación excepcional o cambio muy inusual.

Al 26-oct-2025, Bitcoin cotizaba aproximadamente en 111.000-112.000 $, claramente por encima del umbral de resolución de 93.429,20 $. Reuters y otras fuentes previas al corte muestran que, incluso tras la gran liquidación de octubre, el mínimo reportado fue ~104.783 $, aún por encima del umbral; antes había marcado máximos récord de ~125.246 $. La cuestión se reduce a si BTC caerá más de ~16% desde su nivel de finales de octubre hasta el cierre del 31-dic. Eso es totalmente posible dada su volatilidad, y el mercado de opciones mostraba más coberturas bajistas tras el crash de octubre. Pero también había vientos de cola importantes en 2025: fuerte demanda, récords recientes, victorias regulatorias en EE.UU. y volatilidad implícita relativamente contenida (alta-30s / baja-40s anualizada). Con ~2 meses por delante, un modelo aproximado con esa volatilidad y precio spot actual da una probabilidad en torno a 80-85% de terminar por encima del umbral. Ajustando algo a la baja por el riesgo de una corrección cripto brusca de fin de año, estimo 81%.

By the Oct. 26 cutoff, Erin’s 160 mph peak had not been exceeded, and only about five weeks remained before Dec. 1. Contemporary outlooks indicated no Atlantic tropical cyclone formation expected over the next 7 days, which removes a large chunk of the remaining window. Climatology is strongly against an ultra-intense late-season Atlantic hurricane: only one November Atlantic Category 5 is on record, and >160 mph is an even stricter bar than merely reaching Category 5. Although 2025 was an active/favorable season overall, the combination of no imminent systems and extremely rare historical precedent makes YES very unlikely. I would put it below 5% on unconstrained grounds, so I submit the floor-consistent 5%.

Chegg looks genuinely distressed, but a bankruptcy filing before Jan. 1, 2026 still seems unlikely. By Q2 2025 it still had about $114M of cash/investments, positive adjusted EBITDA, aggressive cost cuts, and ongoing strategic-alternatives efforts. The business is deteriorating fast, with steep revenue/subscriber declines and restructuring charges, so insolvency risk is very real over a longer horizon. But the remaining 2026 convert debt appears to mature in 2026 rather than before year-end 2025, and I found no clear pre-cutoff going-concern warning or near-term covenant/default trigger suggesting an immediate filing within the roughly two-month window from the cutoff. So this is meaningfully above a normal large-cap baseline, but still well below 50% for filing before 2026.

As of 2025-10-26, I found no peer-reviewed paper already published with a >=5σ claim of lepton universality violation. The main 2021 R_K anomaly was effectively washed out by LHCb’s 2022 update, so the clearest earlier path to resolution has closed. The remaining notable tensions are mainly in semitauonic observables like R(D) and R(D*), but sources around 2024–2025 still describe these at roughly ~3–3.2σ in world averages, and specific 2025 Belle II/LHCb results are consistent with the SM or far below discovery level. With only about two months left until the 2025-12-31 publication deadline, reaching a new >=5σ result and getting it through peer review in time looks extremely unlikely. I therefore put this at the floor-allowed low probability of 5%.

As of 2025-10-26, Sherrill looks like the favorite but not a lock. Recent public polls mostly show her ahead: Rutgers/Eagleton had Sherrill +5 (50-45) on Oct. 22, FDU had her +7 (52-45) on Oct. 17, Quinnipiac had her +6 on Oct. 15, and NJ.com/Stockton was also about +5; the main exception was Emerson’s 43-43 dead heat with many undecideds (search_0_summary, search_1_summary). Broadly, October polling shows Sherrill hovering around 50% and Ciattarelli around 45%, which is consistent with RCP’s roughly 3-4 point average edge and a consensus “Lean Democrat” race rating from Cook/Sabato (search_1_summary). Fundamentals also modestly favor Sherrill: New Jersey still has a very large Democratic registration advantage, and Trump lost the state by about 6 points in 2024, though Republicans have made notable registration gains and Ciattarelli already proved in 2021 that he can run competitively statewide (search_2_summary). The main downside risk is turnout/enthusiasm in an off-year election: several reports note Ciattarelli may have the more energized base, and off-year electorates can be older and less predictable. Still, with nearly all late polls showing a Sherrill lead and the state’s underlying partisan lean, I put her win probability at about 79%.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the strongest evidence is the Treasury’s final Monthly Treasury Statement for FY2025 and contemporaneous analyses of it. Those sources put FY2025 customs duties at about $195B, with customs duties up roughly $118B y/y from about $77B in FY2024 (search_0_summary). That is far below the $500B lower bound for this bracket. The resolving source is the FY2025 Financial Report, but historically that report does not revise a category like customs duties upward by anything remotely close to the >$300B needed to cross $500B. Also, if the FY2025 Financial Report were somehow not published by 2026-02-28, the market resolves to the lowest bracket, which also implies NO here. So the only path to YES would be an extraordinary and very implausible discrepancy between the final FY2025 Treasury Statement and the later Financial Report. I assign the minimum allowed probability.

Anthropic looks like a serious contender but not the favorite. The key evidence is that by early October 2025, reports/snippets indicated Claude Sonnet 4.5 was #1 on LMArena, so Anthropic appears to have had the lead shortly before the forecast date. That matters because the horizon to Dec. 31 is only about two months, and the market resolves on the same Arena leaderboard. Anthropic also benefits from the alphabetical tiebreak versus most major rivals (Google, OpenAI, xAI, etc.), which modestly increases its YES chances if scores are very close. Against that, 2025 leadership appears highly volatile: Gemini 2.5 Pro was reportedly #1 in spring with a large jump, GPT-5 was described as leading in August, and top labs were shipping frequent updates. OpenAI and Google in particular remained extremely strong and likely to release or update models before year-end, so a current Anthropic lead was far from secure. Balancing a probable near-term lead plus favorable tiebreak against a fast-moving competitive field, I estimate Anthropic has about a 44% chance to be on top at the Dec. 31 check.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Johannes Kaiser looks like a clear underdog to win the first-round plurality. Late-October polling consistently places him around 12-13.4%, in fourth place, well behind Jeannette Jara (~29.5-32.7%) and José Antonio Kast (~20.1-23.3%), and roughly tied with or slightly behind Evelyn Matthei and Franco Parisi (search_1_summary, search_2_summary, search_3_summary). To finish first, Kaiser would need an unusually large late swing in just about three weeks, plus simultaneous underperformance from both Jara and Kast. That is possible but unlikely. Earlier in 2025 Kaiser briefly polled much better and even near the lead in some snapshots, so I do not put his chances near zero; Chile’s race has been volatile and the right is fragmented. Still, given the size of his current deficit and the short horizon, I estimate only a small chance that he ultimately tops the first-round vote.

Brooklyn looked like one of the NBA’s weakest teams as of 2025-10-26. Multiple previews/projectors had them at roughly 18-24 wins, 14th/15th in the East, and in a clear bottom tier. Sportsbooks listed them around +50000 to win the East, with only slim playoff/play-in chances. The roster is very young, development-focused, and reports suggested the franchise was prioritizing long-term rebuilding and might even move veterans at the deadline. Even with some East rivals weakened by injuries, Brooklyn still projected well below the conference’s true contenders (Cavs, Knicks, Magic, etc.). Their true chance is likely well below 5%, so I’m submitting the minimum allowed probability.

Moonshot’s Kimi K2 clearly had a real shot earlier in the year: reporting in July said it reached the top of LMArena, which means Moonshot is not a fringe candidate. But the later evidence is less favorable. By early September, coverage described Kimi K2 as top-ten and stronger than DeepSeek, while also implying it was no longer ahead of the very best commercial models. Mid-October evidence points to the leaderboard being contested by Google/OpenAI/xAI/Anthropic models, with no direct indication that Moonshot had re-taken #1 near the cutoff. Since the forecast date is only five days before resolution, the key question is basically whether Moonshot is already at or very near the top by Oct. 26; the available evidence does not show that. I give some residual chance because Arena rankings are volatile and Kimi already proved it can hit #1, but overall Moonshot looks like an underdog rather than the modal leader. Tie-breaks are also not ideal for Moonshot versus companies like Anthropic or Google.

Section 4 invocation by Dec. 31, 2025 looks extremely unlikely as of Oct. 26, 2025. The process has never succeeded in US history, requires JD Vance plus a majority of the Cabinet, and if Trump contests it, a 2/3 vote in both chambers of Congress. Reporting up to the cutoff shows public and Democratic calls for using the 25th Amendment, but no strong evidence of an active Vance/Cabinet move; instead, Trump’s 2025 Cabinet is widely described as loyalty-screened and strongly aligned with him. Congress is also Republican-controlled, making any contested removal nearly impossible. If Trump were to suffer genuine medical incapacity in the remaining weeks of 2025, a voluntary Section 3 transfer, resignation, or other route would likely be more plausible than a Section 4 ouster, and those would not resolve YES. So the chance is very low; I assign the minimum allowed 5%.

By the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff, Russia had increased infrastructure and force posture near Finland and some intelligence warnings suggested possible future NATO-testing provocations, including ‘little green men’-style actions. But the strongest contemporaneous evidence still pointed to no immediate threat: Finnish leaders repeatedly said there was no immediate military danger, NATO officials described the buildup as minor/early-stage and not comparable to the pre-2022 Ukraine invasion, and much of the Russian effort appeared oriented toward longer-term reconstitution after Ukraine rather than a near-term assault. A true invasion or territorial seizure of Finland would mean attacking a NATO member and risking major escalation, while Russia remained heavily committed in Ukraine. With only about two months left in 2025, the probability of any qualifying offensive to seize Finnish territory looked low, though not negligible due to hybrid-incursion tail risk.

As of 2025-10-26, the latest available BLS/Reuters-reported headline CPI is 3.0% y/y for September 2025, up only slightly from 2.9% in August (search_1_summary). Earlier 2025 readings cited in Reuters were also far below 4%: 2.9% in Dec 2024 and 3.0% in Jan 2025 (search #5). So Jan-Sep 2025 had not come close to the 4.1% resolution threshold.

That leaves only Oct, Nov, and Dec 2025 as realistic paths to YES. October looks very unlikely because moving from 3.0% in September to a published 4.1%+ in October would require an exceptionally large one-month jump. November is also a stretch. December is the main remaining route, helped by relatively soft late-2024 base effects (Oct 2024 y/y 2.6%, Nov 2.7%, Dec 2.9%; search #4 and #5), but even then inflation would need to accelerate by roughly another full percentage point from the September y/y pace in just three reports.

There is some upside risk from tariffs and pass-through to goods prices, and forecasters were expecting some late-2025 acceleration. But the most recent September report came in slightly below expectations, Reuters noted services inflation remained contained, and BLS data-collection disruptions/government-shutdown issues may reduce the chance of a clean upside surprise (search_1_summary). Balancing those factors, YES is possible but clearly less likely than NO.

As of 2025-10-26, Kansas City is still a serious contender but not an overwhelming one. The evidence I found has them at 4-3 after Week 7, one game behind Denver in the AFC West, but with strong recent momentum (4 wins in 5), key offensive reinforcements back (notably Rashee Rice, plus Xavier Worthy healthy), and betting markets making them the Super Bowl favorite around +500 as of Oct. 20. That price implies roughly a mid-teens true title chance after adjusting for sportsbook margin. Offsetting that: they are not leading their division, their early-season efficiency/DVOA was only middling, and the AFC remains stacked. Putting it together, a fair estimate is about a 15% chance they go on to win Super Bowl LX.

Matt Salsberg is a legitimate contender, but this is still a 64-player single-elimination heads-up event, so win probabilities stay small. A random entrant would be about 1.6%. Salsberg deserves an upgrade from that baseline because he is an accomplished pro (former WPT POY, millions in earnings) and had a notable 2025 win at the WSOP Circuit Commerce Main Event, where he also closed out heads-up play well. Against that, the field is loaded with elite heads-up-capable pros, and his visible bracket path looked tough: Johnny Vibes first, then likely Brian Rast or Ren Lin, with players like Sam Soverel/Jonathan Jaffe in the same section. I found no reliable pre-cutoff reporting that he had already won or made a deep run, so I treat this as mostly an ex-ante skill/bracket forecast. Net: better than a random entrant, but still a low-probability outcome; I place him at the floor of 5%.

As of 2025-10-26, Avatar: Fire and Ash looks much more like a Best Picture nomination contender than a likely winner. Multiple October 2025 Oscar-prediction sources place it around the back half of a plausible top-10 nominee lineup (roughly 7th or omitted from some top 10s), while the race is led by films like One Battle After Another, Hamnet, Sinners, Sentimental Value, and Wicked: For Good. The franchise’s first two films were nominated, so a nomination is quite plausible, and James Cameron’s technical achievements give it a real lane. But historically the Academy is reluctant to give Best Picture to effects-driven sci-fi sequels, and even supportive pundits mainly project it for technical categories rather than as the frontrunner. Since the market resolves No immediately if it misses a nomination, and its conditional win chance even if nominated appears low, I put the overall win probability at the floor: 5%.

By 2025-10-26, the available tracking strongly points away from Eminem. Independent 2025 Spotify-streaming summaries consistently have Bad Bunny and Taylor Swift leading the year, with Drake/The Weeknd also ahead; one Chartmasters-based summary put Bad Bunny at ~21.0B 2025 streams and Taylor at ~20.6B, while Eminem was described as only around 7th overall / 2nd rapper in 2025 tracking. I found no solid evidence of a major late-2025 Eminem release or other catalyst strong enough to close a multi-billion-stream gap in the short remaining Wrapped window. Since Spotify’s official annual #1 has historically come from the current top streaming tier, Eminem’s chances look extremely small.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, there is no evidence that Article 5 had been invoked in 2025. Official NATO and major-media reporting instead show Estonia (and Poland) invoking Article 4 in September after Russian airspace/drone incidents, while Reuters/AP explicitly note Article 5 had still not been triggered and that recent Russian hybrid actions remained below the threshold of open warfare. NATO officials were responding with deterrence, consultations, and enhanced defenses, not collective-defense activation. With only about two months left in 2025 and Article 5 historically invoked only once, the remaining path to YES would require a major sudden escalation—most plausibly a clear armed attack on NATO territory or an unusually severe hybrid/cyber event that NATO collectively treats as equivalent. That tail risk is real given ongoing Russia-related tensions, but still low. I therefore put the probability at the floor-consistent low end: 5%.

Billie Eilish looked like a strong but not leading contender as of 2025-10-26. The best pre-cutoff evidence says: (1) in Spotify Wrapped 2024 she was only #5 globally, even though HIT ME HARD AND SOFT was the #2 album and Birds of a Feather was the #3 song; that suggests that even near a peak cycle, her total artist streams still trailed several bigger-catalog rivals; (2) 2025 proxy indicators before the cutoff, like monthly-listener standings and non-official streaming rankings, consistently pointed more toward Taylor Swift, Bad Bunny, The Weeknd, and sometimes Bruno Mars/Lady Gaga as the biggest Spotify forces, not Billie; and (3) by late October, most of the 2025 Wrapped counting period was effectively already in the books, so a late reversal was unlikely without evidence she was already ahead. Billie clearly had durable streaming momentum, so YES is not impossible, but she did not appear to be the frontrunner. I therefore put her chances at about 9%.

As of 2025-10-26, OpenAI had not officially declared AGI, and the strongest recent evidence cuts against a near-term announcement: after GPT-5’s August 2025 launch, Sam Altman explicitly said OpenAI had not yet achieved AGI and that GPT-5 fell short. Earlier 2025 statements were more bullish—Altman said OpenAI knew how to build AGI and suggested it could arrive this year—but those were forward-looking, not declarations of success. OpenAI’s own messaging also emphasizes that the transition to AGI will not happen all at once, and the company increasingly talks about an 'Intelligence Age' or superintelligence rather than making a discrete AGI claim. That matters because this market resolves on an official announcement, not just rapid capability gains. There is some upside risk from flexible AGI definitions, Altman’s optimism, and reported strategic/legal incentives around OpenAI’s AGI clause with Microsoft. But by late October only about two months remained, and OpenAI had major reasons to avoid a blunt AGI declaration unless it was very clear internally. Net: possible, but unlikely.

Monad had clearly moved into pre-launch mode by the cutoff: the official MON airdrop announcement was live on Oct. 14, 2025, and users could verify eligibility/connect wallets through Nov. 3 (lookup_0, search_2_summary). But the evidence also said the actual airdrop had not happened yet, tokens were not yet transferable, and distribution would occur at TGE/mainnet rather than during the eligibility phase (search_2_summary, search_3_summary). Crucially, as of Oct. 26, the team still had not disclosed a specific mainnet/TGE date, and reporting indicated that date would be announced only after the Nov. 3 claim window closed, with launch broadly expected by end-2025 rather than explicitly by November (search_2_summary, search_3_summary). So YES depends mainly on whether Monad compresses announcement + TGE/mainnet + transferable-token airdrop into the under-4-week window after Nov. 3. That is plausible given the portal is already open, but less than even given the missing date, unknown tokenomics, and repeated reporting framing launch as later/end-of-year. Net: roughly 41%.

As of 2025-10-26, there is no evidence of a novel coronavirus causing sustained human-to-human transmission on a multinational scale, which would be a prerequisite for any WHO statement resembling a pandemic declaration. The main cited candidate, HKU5-CoV-2, was a bat-virus lab finding with no reported human cases and experts explicitly said it was not a current public-health threat. The only notable non-SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus activity was sporadic MERS in Saudi Arabia, with small case counts and limited hospital clusters; WHO said these reports did not change its overall moderate risk assessment. WHO in 2025 also appears focused on long-term coronavirus threat management rather than emergency pandemic labeling, and historically uses the term 'pandemic' sparingly. With only about two months remaining in 2025 and no sign of a qualifying outbreak by the cutoff, the chance of WHO officially announcing a new coronavirus pandemic before year-end is extremely low; I set it at the floor-consistent 5%.

As of 2025-10-26, the best available evidence suggested October 2025 would likely be near, but somewhat above, the 1.10°C NASA GISTEMP threshold. Key points: (1) 2025 had remained exceptionally warm all year and was widely assessed as on track for the 2nd or 3rd warmest year on record; (2) September 2025 was reported by Copernicus as the third-warmest September on record and less than 0.1°C cooler than September 2024, implying late-2025 warmth was still unusually elevated; (3) ENSO was neutral with some chance of weak La Niña developing, which modestly favors cooling but not a sharp drop; (4) a direct NASA-linked May 2025 anomaly was 1.07°C vs 1951-1980, showing the threshold was plausible, but subsequent evidence indicated persistent warmth into September. Overall, October 2025 looked close enough to 1.10°C that a below-threshold outcome was quite possible, but the balance of evidence leaned toward October ending at or above 1.10°C. Hence I estimate about a 34% chance of YES (<1.10°C).

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Kansas City is still very live but no longer clearly favored. Evidence before the cutoff shows: preseason AFC West markets made the Chiefs only a modest favorite (roughly -115 to -122, implying around a 50% normalized division chance); by Oct. 19-23 the Broncos had moved to 5-2 while the Chiefs were 4-3, with the Chargers also 4-3 but fading due to injuries, and the Raiders largely out. The Chiefs were surging and still looked like one of the two best teams in the division, with strong offensive efficiency and Mahomes/Reid’s track record, but they were chasing Denver by a game and had already lost at least one important divisional/tiebreak game (to the Chargers). So the race looked roughly like Broncos slightly ahead, Chiefs close behind, Chargers an outside threat. Net: Kansas City’s chance to win the AFC West is a bit below 50%, about 44%.

As of 2025-10-26, the first leg is already satisfied: the Fed cut in September to 4.00%-4.25% (search_0_summary, search_1_summary). The key issue is the October meeting. Evidence before the meeting overwhelmingly pointed to another cut, not a pause: Reuters reported 115 of 117 economists expecting a 25 bp October cut, futures implied near-100% certainty by Oct. 24, and even earlier CME/FedWatch-style pricing showed roughly 98% odds of an October cut (search_1_summary, search_2_summary). December was also widely expected to bring another cut, with market odds around 90-95% for a further easing if October cut occurred (search_1_summary, search_2_summary). So the only path to YES requires a low-probability surprise pause in October followed by a cut in December. Even if December cut after an October pause would still be plausible, the October pause itself looked very unlikely. My unconstrained estimate would be below 5%, but per instructions I submit the floor-consistent 5%.

As of the cutoff, Moonshot looks like a credible frontier contender but still a clear long shot to be #1 on Chatbot Arena by Dec. 31. The strongest direct evidence is that Kimi-K2 was reported around Sep. 10, 2025 as only tied for 8th place on LMArena, behind several leading US models, with Qwen also ahead among Chinese models (search_3_summary). That means Moonshot was competitive but not especially close to the top at the latest concrete leaderboard evidence I found. On the positive side, Moonshot has substantial funding, strong technical talent, and Kimi K2 was described as globally competitive / leading among open-source models, with strong coding and agentic performance (search_5_summary). So a late-2025 jump via a new release is plausible. But they would need to overtake multiple very strong rivals (OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, xAI, etc.) within about two months, and tie-break rules are unfavorable because Moonshot would lose first-place ties to several alphabetically earlier companies. Net: meaningful but low chance, about 9%.

September 2025 was still exceptionally warm globally (Copernicus: third-warmest September on record, only slightly below 2024), and 2025 was tracking among the three warmest years overall. At the same time, ENSO was neutral with some weak La Niña tendency, which argues against the very highest anomalies seen during stronger El Niño influence. A known NASA GISTEMP point from May 2025 was 1.07°C above 1951-1980, and the late-2025 background suggests October should likely land somewhat above that, plausibly around the low-to-mid 1.1s on NASA’s baseline. The 1.10-1.14°C band looks near the plausible center but it is only a 0.05°C-wide bracket, so it should get a substantial minority rather than majority of the probability mass. I estimate about a 30% chance.

By Oct. 26, 2025, the evidence overwhelmingly favored a 25 bps cut at the Oct. 28–29 FOMC meeting. Market pricing was around 97–99% for a quarter-point cut, with only tiny odds on no change or 50 bps. A Reuters poll found 115 of 117 economists expecting exactly a 25 bps cut, and multiple Fed officials were reported as comfortable with further easing, including Waller explicitly backing an October cut. The September dot plot also pointed to additional 2025 easing. The main uncertainty was not the direction but the exact size: inflation had firmed somewhat, the committee was split between inflation and labor concerns, and a government shutdown created a data vacuum that could have made the Fed more cautious or, less likely, more aggressive if conditions deteriorated suddenly. Given the very strong consensus but allowing for residual event risk and committee discretion, I estimate a 95% chance of an exactly 25 bps cut.

As of 2025-10-26, Mamdani is very unlikely to finish second. Multiple late polls consistently have him in first by double digits, with Cuomo in second and Sliwa in third: e.g. Quinnipiac ~46-33-15, Fox ~52-28-14, AARP/Gotham ~43-29-19, Emerson ~43-28-10 (search_1_summary, search_2_summary, search_0_summary). The strongest anti-Mamdani case is that if Sliwa were gone, Cuomo could consolidate and make it close, but Sliwa is still in the race and Cuomo himself described Sliwa as a spoiler (search_1_summary, search_3_summary). Early voting also appeared relatively strong in Mamdani-friendly areas (search_3_summary). So the modal outcome is Mamdani 1st, Cuomo 2nd. For YES to resolve, Mamdani would need a substantial late collapse while still staying ahead of Sliwa—possible, but only a small tail risk.

Le Havre look like an extreme long shot as of 2025-10-26. Preseason/early-season models had them around 16th with only ~33.5 expected points and a substantial relegation risk, while PSG were the clear title favorites and Monaco/Marseille the main challengers. Contemporary previews described Le Havre as a survival-level side with modest recruitment, not a contender. By late October they had won just once in league play and were coming off a 6-2 loss at Marseille, which is far from title-winning form. Their true chance is likely below 5%, but given the forecasting constraints I assign the minimum allowed probability.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Walter Reeves looked extremely unlikely to win. Early-year coverage mentioned him as a potential challenger, but the strongest later evidence cuts the other way: official Atlanta Municipal Clerk qualifying summaries did not list Reeves among qualified mayoral candidates, while listing Andre Dickens and other candidates (search_2_summary). October candidate guides also omitted Reeves from the mayoral field and instead described a small set of other challengers to Dickens (search_0_summary). On top of that, Dickens had the normal incumbent advantage plus a very large fundraising edge and local coverage framed challengers as facing an uphill battle (search_1_summary). If Reeves was not officially qualified / not on the ballot, his win chances were effectively nil; even allowing for some uncertainty in the reporting, the probability is at the floor.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the best evidence points to xAI trailing rather than leading on Chatbot Arena. Search evidence indicates Gemini 2.5 Pro took #1 in March by a large margin, Grok 4 was only around #4 in mid-July, and GPT-5 was reported as topping LMArena in early August. By late September the field still looked like a volatile OpenAI/Google/Anthropic/xAI race, but xAI was not the incumbent leader. xAI does have a credible upside path: Musk said Grok 5 would launch before year-end, and a strong late release could still reach #1.
